retrieved_docs.title: Diabetic Nephropathy -- Toxicity and Adverse Effect Management -- Effect of Chronic Kidney Disease on Diabetes Medications
retrieved_docs.content: Metformin is contraindicated in patients with an eGFR of less than 30 mL/min/1.73m 2 due to the increased risk of lactic acidosis. For most oral antidiabetic medications, caution is advised when the eGFR is less than 45 mL/min/1.73m 2 .

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin-Associated Lactic Acidosis (MALA) -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Hemodialysis should be initiated for pH < 7.0, lactate > 20, and in patients who do not respond to more conservative therapies

retrieved_docs.title: Diabetes -- Toxicity and Adverse Effect Management
retrieved_docs.content: One of the most common adverse effects of insulin is hypoglycemia. Gastrointestinal upset is the most common side effect of many of the T2DM medications. Metformin can lead to lactic acidosis and should be used with caution in patients with renal disease and discontinued if the estimated glomerular filtration rate (e-GFR) is under 30 mL/min. Sulfonylureas can lead to hypoglycemia and may promote cardiovascular death in patients with diabetes. [35] Thiazolidinediones have fallen out of favor in clinical practice due to their adverse effects, specifically resulting in fluid retention, worsening heart failure, and fractures. [36] [37] DPP-4 may increase the risk for upper respiratory tract infections but may have less nausea and diarrhea compared to other drugs such as metformin. [38] [39] SGLT-2 inhibitors can lead to increased urinary tract infections due to increased urinary glucose excretion. [40] Both SGLT2 inhibitors and GLP-1 Receptor agonists reduce ASCVD events and are now considered the second line to metformin in such patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin-Associated Lactic Acidosis (MALA) -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Patients presenting with MALA may exhibit signs of shock requiring aggressive management, including intubation, ventilation, and vasopressor support

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin-Associated Lactic Acidosis (MALA) -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Metformin is a biguanide compound used as an antihyperglycemic in the treatment of diabetes mellitus

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin-Associated Lactic Acidosis (MALA) -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with symptoms or evidence of MALA should be admitted. If the acidosis is severe, they require bicarbonate or extracorporeal renal clearance or require vasopressor or ventilator support; they should be admitted to the ICU. Patients who remain asymptomatic and do not have evidence of MALA on evaluation during an observational period of at least 12 hours can be safely discharged.

retrieved_docs.title: Lactic Acidosis -- Pearls and Other Issues -- Prevention
retrieved_docs.content: High-risk medications should be avoided in vulnerable populations (eg, diabetic patients on metformin and patients with hepatic or renal impairment). Lactate levels should be monitored in individuals at risk during acute illness. Early recognition and resuscitation in shock states are essential to prevent progression to severe acidosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin-Associated Lactic Acidosis (MALA) -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Metformin-associated lactic acidosis is an important topic for practitioners of different specialties, including emergency physicians, internists, critical care physicians, toxicologists, pharmacists, and nephrologists. A multidisciplinary approach is required to stabilize patients during the acute toxicity phase. Patients with concerns for MALA should have the following: Obtain and monitor vital signs Contact your local Poison Control Center for assistance with management and epidemiology collection. Extensive laboratory assessment to search for additional and alternative causes of patient’s presentation. Aggressive treatment of severe acidosis with initiation sodium bicarbonate and hemodialysis when indicated.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 5 -- Community 2962
retrieved_docs.content: Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) is clinically associated with type 2 diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري نوع 2) and with reduced blood volume (قلة حجم الدم). Type 2 diabetes can predispose patients to acute kidney injury through chronic hyperglycemia‑induced vascular and tubular damage, making the kidneys more vulnerable to hemodynamic stress. Conversely, hypovolemia decreases renal perfusion and can directly precipitate acute kidney failure. Recognizing that both type 2 diabetes and low intravascular volume are important clinical contributors to acute kidney failure guides clinicians to monitor diabetic patients closely, ensure adequate hydration, and intervene promptly to preserve renal function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1752
retrieved_docs.content: Magnesium gluconate is contraindicated in patients with قصور الكلى (renal failure), meaning its use should be avoided in individuals suffering from kidney failure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 5 -- Community 2925
retrieved_docs.content: Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) is clinically associated with both type 1 diabetes (مرض السكري نوع 1) and insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes (مرض السكري من النوع الثاني (2) المتطلب للأنسولين), indicating that patients with these forms of diabetes are at increased risk for developing sudden renal impairment. Dehydration (التجفاف) is also clinically linked to acute kidney failure, serving as a common precipitating factor that can exacerbate renal dysfunction in diabetic individuals. Recognizing the interplay among acute kidney failure, diabetes (both type 1 and insulin‑dependent type 2), and dehydration is essential for early identification, prevention, and management of renal complications in these patient populations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 286
retrieved_docs.content: زيادة الفجوة الأنيونية (increased anion gap) is clinically associated with قصور الكلى (renal failure), indicating that patients with renal failure often exhibit a widened anion gap in laboratory assessments. Consequently, magnesium gluconate is contraindicated in individuals with قصور الكلى, meaning that the use of magnesium gluconate should be avoided in patients suffering from renal failure to prevent potential adverse effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 141

Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) co‑occurs with diabetes, acute pancreatitis (الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياسِ الحادّ), dehydration (التجفاف), acidosis, respiratory depression (تَقَلْوُن تنفسي), abnormal mental states, and low blood chloride (نقص كلوريد الدم). Acute pancreatitis also predisposes to acute kidney failure. Hyperkalemia frequently co‑occurs with metabolic acidosis and with hypoglycemia, the latter being linked to the antidiabetic drug metformin (fenformin). Acute kidney injury (AKI) is clinically associated with hyperuricemia, hyperkalemia, hypovolemia, hyponatremia syndrome, and insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes; AKI may progress to uremic toxicity (extrarenal nitrogen accumulation). Urinary tract disorder is a parent condition of AKI and is related to cystic disease (الدَّاءُ السِّيسْتينِي); pyelonephritis is associated with hyponatremia. Increased plasma osmolality arises from dehydration, co‑occurs with hypovolemia, abnormal mental states, and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2898
retrieved_docs.content: Dehydration, abnormal psychological state, and insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes are clinically associated with acute kidney failure. Acute kidney failure often develops in the context of severe dehydration, which can precipitate rapid loss of renal function. Patients with acute kidney failure may also present with abnormal psychological states, reflecting the systemic impact of renal dysfunction on mental status. Moreover, type 2 diabetes that requires insulin therapy is linked to acute kidney failure, indicating that poorly controlled diabetes is a significant comorbidity that can predispose to or worsen renal injury. Recognizing these interconnections aids in comprehensive assessment and management of affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1968
retrieved_docs.content: Fluid and electrolyte disorders are closely linked to the development of diabetic hyperosmolar coma, as disturbances in fluid balance and electrolyte concentrations can precipitate the severe hyperosmolar state that characterizes this life‑threatening complication of diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2467
retrieved_docs.content: Clofibrate (klofrolát) exerts a physiologic effect by decreasing lipolysis, which contributes to its lipid‑lowering action. Because of its pharmacologic profile, clofibrate is contraindicated in patients with renal failure (قصور الكلى), and should be avoided in this population to prevent adverse outcomes.

retrieved_docs.content: with severe outcomes such as foot amputation, above‑knee amputation, extrarenal nitrogenous waste accumulation, and acute kidney failure. Type 1 diabetes is further associated with persistent high plasma osmolality, acute kidney failure, and above‑knee amputation, while type 2 diabetes shares associations with acute kidney failure and high plasma osmolality. Metformin‑induced hypoglycemia can precipitate hyperkalemia. Acute kidney failure co‑occurs with dehydration, metabolic acidosis, respiratory distress, abnormal psychological states, reduced blood volume, hyperuricemia, hyperkalemia, insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes, and leads to secondary urinary‑tract disorders and extrarenal nitrogenous waste accumulation. Acute pancreatitis is related to hypochloremia, and alcoholic liver inflammation contributes to fluid‑electrolyte and acid‑base imbalance. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 221
retrieved_docs.content: This failure state is used to interpret uncontrolled disease‑condition determinations, which are further linked to diabetes mellitus with complications. The summary highlights the hierarchical organization of acute and chronic cardiac manifestations, their multilingual documentation, and the interplay between cardiac, metabolic, gastrointestinal, and renal‑hepatic organ failures, emphasizing the importance of integrated assessment and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 882
retrieved_docs.content: The general clinical state functions as an interpretive framework for both Disease condition determination, uncontrolled and Failure (biologic function). Disease condition determination, uncontrolled is associated with diabetes mellitus with complications, indicating that when a disease’s status is not controlled it frequently co‑exists with complicated diabetes. Failure (biologic function) is also interpreted through the general clinical state and is associated with الفَشَلٌ الكَبِدِيُّ الكُلْوِيّ (hepatorenal failure), highlighting a link between systemic biologic failure and combined liver‑kidney failure. Recognizing that uncontrolled disease conditions and biologic function failures are reflected in the general clinical state helps clinicians assess overall patient status and guide comprehensive management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2899
retrieved_docs.content: Increased plasma osmolality (ارتفاع أسمولالية البلازما) is clinically associated with high blood glucose (ارتفاع السكر في الدم), indicating that hyperglycemia often contributes to or results from elevated osmotic concentration in the blood. This condition is also linked to urinary tract disorders (اضطراب السبيل البولي), suggesting that disturbances of the urinary system can be an underlying or related factor in the development of high plasma osmolality. Additionally, increased plasma osmolality frequently co‑occurs with low blood volume (قلة حجم الدم), reflecting the close interplay between osmotic balance, fluid volume status, and glucose regulation. Recognizing that hyperglycemia, urinary tract pathology, and hypovolemia commonly intersect with elevated plasma osmolality helps clinicians assess underlying causes and guide comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1775
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetic retinopathy (اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ) is clinically associated with end‑stage renal disease (مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية), reflecting the shared microvascular complications of advanced diabetes. Urinary tract infection (عدوى المسالك البولية) is also clinically associated with end‑stage renal disease, indicating that patients with severe kidney failure have an increased risk of infections of the urinary tract. In medical terminology, urinary tract infections are classified as “urinary tract infections” (urinary tract infections → عدوى المسالك البولية), highlighting the equivalence of the English and Arabic designations for the same condition. Lithium toxicity (التسمم بالليثيوم) is clinically associated with urinary tract infection, suggesting that lithium‑related renal effects may predispose patients to such infections. Lithium toxicity is additionally clinically associated with schizophrenia (الفُصام), underscoring the use of lithium in psychiatric treatment and the potential for toxic side effects in patients with this disorder. Recognizing these interconnections helps clinicians monitor renal function, infection risk, and medication safety in individuals with diabetes, kidney disease, or psychiatric conditions.

Together, these relationships illustrate how secondary diabetes mellitus drives specific retinal and renal complications, how severe subtypes may involve peripheral vascular disease and gangrene, and how broader systemic failures—particularly cardiac, hepatic, and renal—interact with the general clinical picture, informing comprehensive patient evaluation and management.

retrieved_docs.content: insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes, indicating that fluid‑osmotic disturbances can precipitate renal deterioration. AKI leads to accumulation of extrarenal nitrogenous waste and is a subcategory of renal insufficiency (قصور الكلى). Acute pancreatitis (الالتهاب الحاد للبنكرياس) is clinically linked to hypochloremia and AKI, suggesting that pancreatic inflammation can precipitate or exacerbate renal failure. Hypoglycemia, as part of other endocrine disorders, is associated with hyperkalemia, demonstrating a glucose‑potassium interaction. Klofibrát (klofibrát) physiologically reduces lipolysis but is contraindicated in renal insufficiency, underscoring the need to avoid it in patients with impaired kidney function. Urinary tract disorders are closely tied to AKI and to elevated plasma osmolality, which often stems from dehydration. An increased anion gap is also associated with renal failure, while pyelonephritis (التهاب الحويضة والكلية) correlates with hyponatremia syndrome. Acidosis

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2157
retrieved_docs.content: Increased plasma osmolality is clinically associated with elevated blood glucose levels (hyperglycemia). This rise in plasma osmolality often originates from dehydration, as fluid loss concentrates solutes in the bloodstream. Additionally, increased plasma osmolality commonly co‑occurs with low blood volume (hypovolemia). Recognizing that dehydration can lead to higher plasma osmolality, which in turn may signal hyperglycemia and accompany hypovolemia, is essential for accurate diagnosis and appropriate fluid and glucose management in patient care.
